name: Pre-Islamic Tribal Arabia
desc: Fractured tribal society of the Arabian Peninsula with no central authority, dominated by inter-tribal warfare, polytheistic religion, and trade through Mecca.

name: Birth of Muhammad
desc: Muhammad ibn Abdullah is born in Mecca into the Banu Hashim clan of the Quraysh tribe, in the Year of the Elephant.

name: Marriage to Khadijah
desc: Muhammad marries Khadijah bint Khuwaylid, a wealthy and respected Meccan merchant, providing him with stability and a partner who would become the first Muslim.

name: First Revelation in Cave of Hira
desc: The angel Jibril (Gabriel) appears to Muhammad in the Cave of Hira, beginning the revelation of the Quran with the command 'Iqra' (Read).

name: Quraysh Persecution of Early Muslims
desc: Quraysh leadership escalates persecution of early Muslims, especially those without tribal protection, including torture, social boycott, and economic pressure.

name: Migration to Abyssinia
desc: A group of early Muslims migrates to Christian Abyssinia (Ethiopia) seeking protection under the Negus, marking Islam's first contact with an African Christian civilization.

name: Boycott of Banu Hashim
desc: The Quraysh impose a three-year social and economic boycott on the Banu Hashim clan, isolating Muhammad and his supporters in the valley of Abu Talib.

name: Year of Sorrow
desc: Muhammad loses both his wife Khadijah and his uncle and protector Abu Talib in the same year, leaving him without his closest emotional support and tribal protection.

name: Night Journey (Isra wal Miraj)
desc: Muhammad's miraculous night journey from Mecca to Jerusalem and ascension through the heavens, establishing Al-Aqsa as a sacred Islamic site.

name: Pledges of Aqaba
desc: Two successive pledges from a delegation of Yathrib (Madinah) tribesmen to support and protect Muhammad, opening the path for the Hijra.

name: Hijra to Madinah
desc: Muhammad and his followers migrate from Mecca to Madinah, establishing the first Islamic polity. The event marks year 1 of the Islamic calendar.

name: Constitution of Madinah
desc: A written compact between Muslims, Jewish tribes, and other clans of Madinah, often considered the first written multi-faith governance document.

name: Change of Qibla to Mecca
desc: The direction of prayer is changed from Jerusalem to the Kaaba in Mecca, asserting a distinct Islamic identity while preserving Jerusalem's earlier sacred status.

name: Battle of Badr
desc: A small Muslim force defeats a much larger Quraysh army, the first major military victory of the nascent Muslim community and a decisive psychological turning point.

name: Battle of Uhud
desc: Quraysh counterattack at Uhud results in a Muslim setback after archers abandon their post, becoming a foundational lesson in discipline and obedience to leadership.

name: Battle of the Trench
desc: On the advice of Salman al-Farsi, Muslims dig a trench around Madinah, repelling a confederate siege using Persian tactics and demonstrating openness to foreign innovation.

name: Treaty of Hudaybiyyah
desc: A ten-year peace treaty with the Quraysh that initially seemed unfavorable but allowed Islam to spread peacefully across Arabia, tripling the Muslim population within two years.

name: Conquest of Mecca
desc: Muhammad enters Mecca with minimal bloodshed and grants general amnesty to former enemies, transforming many into loyal Muslims and unifying Arabia.

name: Farewell Sermon
desc: On the Plain of Arafat, Muhammad delivers a sermon affirming racial equality, the rights of women, sanctity of life and property, and the completion of the religion.

name: Death of the Prophet
desc: Muhammad dies in Madinah, triggering the first major succession crisis in Islamic history.

name: Preservation of the Sunnah in Hadith
desc: Systematic compilation of the Prophet's sayings and actions by scholars like Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, and others, establishing Hadith as the second source of Islamic law.

name: Caliphate of Abu Bakr
desc: Abu Bakr is elected as the first caliph through consultation at Saqifah, holding the community together at its most fragile moment.

name: Ridda (Apostasy) Wars
desc: Abu Bakr suppresses tribes that refused to pay zakat or claimed prophethood after Muhammad's death, reuniting Arabia under Islamic authority.

name: Battle of Yamama
desc: Decisive battle of the Ridda Wars against the false prophet Musaylimah, in which a large number of Quran memorizers are killed.

name: First Compilation of the Quran
desc: Concerned by the loss of huffadh at Yamama, Abu Bakr commissions Zayd ibn Thabit to compile the Quran in a single written manuscript.

name: Caliphate of Umar ibn al-Khattab
desc: Umar's ten-year reign produces the most rapid territorial expansion in early history, plus institutional foundations for Islamic governance.

name: Battle of Yarmouk
desc: Decisive Muslim victory over the Byzantine Empire that effectively ended Byzantine rule over Syria and Palestine.

name: Battle of al-Qadisiyyah
desc: Muslim victory that broke the back of the Sassanid Persian Empire and opened Mesopotamia to Islamic rule.

name: Muslim Conquest of Jerusalem
desc: Umar personally accepts the surrender of Jerusalem from Patriarch Sophronius, entering the city humbly on foot.

name: Covenant of Umar
desc: A pact protecting the lives, property, and churches of Jerusalem's Christians, setting a precedent for Islamic governance of the Holy City for centuries.

name: Muslim Conquest of Egypt
desc: Amr ibn al-As leads the conquest of Byzantine Egypt, opening North Africa and securing the breadbasket of the empire.

name: Battle of Nahavand
desc: Final destruction of the Sassanid Persian Empire, ending over four centuries of Sassanid rule and bringing Persian civilization into the Islamic world.

name: Founding of Garrison Cities
desc: Umar founds Basra, Kufa, and Fustat as garrison cities (amsar) to manage the new conquests; they grow into major centers of Islamic civilization.

name: Establishment of the Diwan
desc: Umar institutes a state register paying stipends to Muslims based on length of service in Islam, an early precursor to the welfare state.

name: Establishment of the Hijri Calendar
desc: Umar formally adopts the Hijra as year one of the Islamic calendar, giving the Muslim world a unified sense of time.

name: Assassination of Umar
desc: Umar is stabbed during Fajr prayer by a Persian slave, Abu Lulu. Before dying he appoints a six-man council (shura) to choose his successor.

name: Caliphate of Uthman ibn Affan
desc: Uthman's twelve-year reign sees major standardization of the Quran, the first Islamic navy, and growing dissent over alleged nepotism.

name: Standardization of the Mushaf
desc: Uthman commissions a single authoritative recension of the Quran and orders variant manuscripts destroyed; this Mushaf is the textual basis of every Quran today.

name: First Islamic Navy
desc: Under Uthman, Muawiya builds the first Islamic navy, defeating Byzantine fleets at the Battle of the Masts (Dhat al-Sawari) and opening Mediterranean power.

name: Assassination of Uthman
desc: Rebels from Egypt and Iraq besiege Uthman in Madinah and kill him while he reads the Quran, opening the First Fitna (civil war).

name: Caliphate of Ali ibn Abi Talib
desc: The fourth caliph's reign is consumed by internal conflict, with the Muslim community split for the first time over questions of legitimacy and justice.

name: Battle of the Camel
desc: First open battle between Muslim armies, fought near Basra between Ali's forces and those of Aisha, Talha, and Zubayr who demanded justice for Uthman.

name: Battle of Siffin
desc: Indecisive battle between Ali and Muawiya that ended in arbitration, fracturing Ali's coalition and producing the Kharijite movement.

name: Emergence of the Kharijites
desc: A faction of Ali's army secedes after rejecting the Siffin arbitration, declaring all who disagreed with them apostates. Islam's first sectarian extremist movement.

name: Assassination of Ali
desc: Ali is killed in Kufa by the Kharijite Ibn Muljam, ending the Rashidun era and clearing the path for Umayyad rule.

name: Founding of the Umayyad Caliphate
desc: Muawiya consolidates power and moves the capital to Damascus, transforming the caliphate from elective consultation into hereditary monarchy.

name: Martyrdom of Husayn at Karbala
desc: Husayn ibn Ali and his small party are killed by Umayyad forces near Karbala in Iraq, becoming the central tragedy and rallying event of Shia Islam.

name: Sunni-Shia Split Solidified
desc: The political and theological dispute over leadership crystallizes into two distinct traditions whose differences would shape Islamic history permanently.

name: Construction of the Dome of the Rock
desc: Caliph Abd al-Malik builds the Dome of the Rock on the Temple Mount, the first major work of Islamic architecture and a statement of Jerusalem's centrality to Islam.

name: Arabic Becomes Administrative Language
desc: Abd al-Malik standardizes Arabic as the language of administration and reforms the coinage, unifying the empire linguistically and economically.

name: Completion of Al-Aqsa Mosque
desc: Caliph al-Walid I completes the Al-Aqsa Mosque on the Haram al-Sharif, anchoring the Noble Sanctuary as a permanent center of Islamic worship.

name: Tariq ibn Ziyad Invades Iberia
desc: The Berber commander Tariq crosses the strait that bears his name (Jabal Tariq, Gibraltar) with about 7,000 troops, beginning the conquest of the Iberian Peninsula.

name: Collapse of the Visigothic Kingdom
desc: King Roderick is defeated at the Battle of Guadalete after being betrayed by rival Visigothic factions; the kingdom collapses within seven years.

name: Muhammad bin Qasim Conquers Sindh
desc: An Umayyad expedition under the seventeen-year-old Muhammad bin Qasim conquers Sindh, planting Islam in the Indian subcontinent for the first time.

name: Battle of Tours
desc: Frankish forces under Charles Martel halt the northward Muslim expansion in Gaul, redirecting Andalusian energy inward toward urban culture and learning.

name: Abbasid Revolution
desc: A coalition of Persians, Shia, and disaffected Arabs under the Abbasid family overthrows the Umayyads, massacring most of the family at a banquet near Damascus.

name: Battle of Talas
desc: Abbasid forces defeat a Tang Chinese army in Central Asia. Captured Chinese paper-makers reveal the secret of paper production to the Islamic world.

name: Abd al-Rahman I Escapes to Iberia
desc: The last surviving Umayyad prince flees the Abbasid massacre, crosses North Africa, and reaches Iberia after years on the run.

name: Emirate of Cordoba Established
desc: Abd al-Rahman I establishes an independent Umayyad emirate in Cordoba, beginning a separate, distinctly Andalusian branch of Islamic civilization.

name: Baghdad Founded
desc: Caliph al-Mansur founds the Round City of Baghdad on the Tigris, deliberately positioned to fuse Persian, Greek, Indian, and Arab knowledge networks.

name: Great Mosque of Cordoba Begun
desc: Abd al-Rahman I begins construction of the Great Mosque, fusing Roman columns, Visigothic horseshoe arches, and Islamic design into a new architectural language.

name: Reign of Harun al-Rashid
desc: The peak of early Abbasid wealth and prestige, with diplomatic exchanges reaching as far as the court of Charlemagne and laying groundwork for translation-era patronage.

name: Paper Industry Established
desc: Paper mills are established in Baghdad and other cities, replacing parchment and papyrus and making books dramatically cheaper to produce and circulate.

name: Establishment of Bimaristans
desc: Public hospitals (bimaristans) are established across the Abbasid realm with separate wards, free care regardless of faith or means, and clinical training for students.

name: Caliphate of Al-Mamun
desc: Al-Mamun's reign formalizes state patronage of the sciences and translation, making Baghdad the unrivaled intellectual capital of the world.

name: Al-Khwarizmi Develops Algebra
desc: Al-Khwarizmi's Kitab al-Jabr wal-Muqabala founds algebra as a discipline. His name gives us 'algorithm' and his methods underlie modern computing.

name: Adoption of Hindu-Arabic Numerals
desc: Al-Khwarizmi and others systematize the Indian decimal place-value system, including zero, transforming arithmetic and enabling the modern world of calculation.

name: House of Wisdom Established
desc: Al-Mamun develops the Bayt al-Hikma in Baghdad as a major translation and research institution, employing Christian, Jewish, Zoroastrian, and Muslim scholars side by side.

name: Translation of Greek Classics into Arabic
desc: Aristotle, Plato, Galen, Euclid, and Ptolemy are translated into Arabic, often by Christian scholars like Hunayn ibn Ishaq, preserving works that Europe had largely lost.

name: Al-Razi's Medical Innovations
desc: The Persian physician Abu Bakr al-Razi distinguishes smallpox from measles, introduces clinical case studies, and challenges Galenic dogma with empirical observation.

name: Fatimid Caliphate Established
desc: An Ismaili Shia dynasty proclaims itself caliphate in Ifriqiya, eventually conquering Egypt and challenging both Abbasid and Andalusian Sunni authority.

name: Abd al-Rahman III Declares Caliphate of Cordoba
desc: The Cordoban emir takes the title of caliph in response to the Fatimid challenge, formalizing Cordoba as the third caliphate of the Islamic world.

name: Cordoba's Zenith
desc: Cordoba reaches an estimated half-million inhabitants with paved and lit streets, public baths, libraries, and a sewer system, becoming the largest city in Europe.

name: Al-Sufi's Book of Fixed Stars
desc: The Persian astronomer Abd al-Rahman al-Sufi catalogues constellations and gives Arabic names to stars (Aldebaran, Betelgeuse, Vega) that astronomy still uses today.

name: Al-Azhar University Founded
desc: The Fatimids found Al-Azhar in their new capital of Cairo, originally as a Shia institution; later a Sunni stronghold and one of the oldest continuously operating universities in the world.

name: Pope Sylvester II Studies Islamic Learning
desc: Gerbert of Aurillac studies in Catalan monasteries fed by Andalusian science. As Pope Sylvester II he champions Arabic numerals and the abacus in Christian Europe.

name: Islamization of the Ghana Empire
desc: Trans-Saharan trade and gradual conversion of Soninke elites carry Islam into West Africa, beginning a centuries-long Islamization of the Sahel.

name: Mahmud of Ghazni's Indian Campaigns
desc: The Ghaznavid sultan launches seventeen raids into northern India, establishing a permanent Muslim political presence and Persianate administrative culture in the subcontinent.

name: Al-Hakim Destroys Church of the Holy Sepulchre
desc: The eccentric Fatimid caliph al-Hakim orders the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem, generating long-lasting outrage in Christian Europe.

name: Ibn al-Haytham's Optics
desc: Ibn al-Haytham (Alhazen) publishes the Kitab al-Manazir, proving light enters the eye, founding modern optics, and articulating an experimental scientific method.

name: Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine
desc: The Persian polymath's Canon synthesizes Greek and Islamic medicine into a fourteen-volume textbook used in European universities until the seventeenth century.

name: Cordoban Caliphate Fragments into Taifas
desc: Civil war and weak succession shatter the Cordoban caliphate into competing taifa kingdoms, opening Iberia to Christian pressure from the north.

name: Battle of Manzikert
desc: The Seljuk Turks defeat the Byzantine Empire and capture Emperor Romanos IV, opening Anatolia to Turkic settlement and ultimately the rise of the Ottomans.

name: Seljuk Settlement of Anatolia
desc: Turkic tribes migrate into Anatolia after Manzikert, gradually transforming the demographic and religious character of what had been a Byzantine heartland.

name: Toledo Falls to Christians
desc: Alfonso VI captures Toledo with its enormous Arabic libraries intact, providing the raw material for the largest knowledge transfer in medieval European history.

name: Almoravid Intervention in Iberia
desc: Yusuf ibn Tashfin and the Almoravids cross from North Africa to defend Andalusi Muslims, defeating Alfonso VI at Sagrajas but later absorbing Al-Andalus into a stricter empire.

name: Pope Urban II Calls First Crusade
desc: At the Council of Clermont, Urban II preaches an armed pilgrimage to recover Jerusalem, channeling European militarism into a religious campaign.

name: First Crusade
desc: European armies traverse the Mediterranean and Anatolia, exploiting the political fragmentation of the Muslim Levant after Manzikert and the Sunni-Shia divide.

name: Crusader Capture and Massacre of Jerusalem
desc: Crusader armies storm Jerusalem and slaughter much of its Muslim and Jewish population over several days, converting Al-Aqsa into a church and the Dome of the Rock into a chapel.

name: Toledo School of Translators
desc: Under Archbishop Raymond and his successors, a multi-faith team in Toledo translates Arabic philosophical, scientific, and medical works into Latin, reshaping European thought.

name: Zangi Unifies Mosul and Aleppo
desc: The Turkic atabeg Imad ad-Din Zangi unites Mosul and Aleppo and recaptures Edessa from the Crusaders, beginning the Muslim counter-mobilization.

name: Nur ad-Din Consolidates Muslim Unity
desc: Zangi's son consolidates Syria and revives institutions of Sunni learning and jihad, mentoring the young Saladin in his court.

name: Almohad Dynasty
desc: A reformist Berber movement displaces the Almoravids and rules a North African and Andalusian empire, supporting philosophers like Ibn Rushd while repressing other groups.

name: Saladin Unites Egypt and Syria
desc: Salah ad-Din ends the Fatimid caliphate, founds the Ayyubid dynasty, and unites Egypt, Syria, and the Hijaz under a single Sunni political order.

name: Ibn Rushd's Aristotelian Commentaries
desc: The Andalusian philosopher Averroes writes the most influential medieval commentaries on Aristotle, decisively shaping later scholasticism through Latin translation.

name: Battle of Hattin
desc: Saladin lures the Crusader field army into the waterless hills of Hattin and annihilates it, breaking the Latin Kingdom of Jerusalem in a single day.

name: Saladin Reconquers Jerusalem
desc: Saladin retakes Jerusalem with mercy and restraint, ransoming prisoners and protecting non-combatants, in deliberate contrast to the Crusader massacre of 1099.

name: Third Crusade
desc: European kings including Richard I and Philip II launch a counter-campaign that recovers a coastal strip but cannot retake Jerusalem.

name: Delhi Sultanate Founded
desc: Qutb-ud-din Aibak establishes the Delhi Sultanate, beginning over three centuries of Turkic and Afghan Muslim rule across much of the Indian subcontinent.

name: Genghis Khan Unifies the Mongols
desc: Temujin is proclaimed Genghis Khan at the kurultai, forging the most efficient military machine the world had seen and setting the stage for unprecedented expansion.

name: Mongol Invasion of Khwarezm
desc: After a Khwarezmian governor murders Mongol envoys, Genghis Khan unleashes a campaign that destroys the Khwarezmian Empire and devastates Persia and Central Asia.

name: Mali Empire Founded
desc: Sundiata Keita defeats the Sosso and founds the Mali Empire, which under his successors becomes a wealthy and increasingly Islamicized West African power.

name: Fall of Cordoba
desc: Ferdinand III of Castile captures Cordoba, converting the Great Mosque into a cathedral and signaling that the Reconquista has reached the heart of Al-Andalus.

name: Nasrid Emirate of Granada
desc: Muhammad I founds the Nasrid emirate of Granada, the last Muslim polity in Iberia, surviving as a vassal of Castile by paying tribute.

name: Alhambra Construction Begins
desc: Construction begins on the Alhambra palace-city of Granada, the final great architectural statement of Andalusian Islam.

name: Ibn al-Nafis Discovers Pulmonary Circulation
desc: Working in Damascus and Cairo, Ibn al-Nafis describes the pulmonary circulation of blood three centuries before William Harvey.

name: Fall of Seville
desc: Ferdinand III also captures Seville, leaving Granada as the only remaining Muslim emirate in the Iberian Peninsula.

name: Mamluk Sultanate Founded
desc: Slave-soldier (mamluk) commanders seize power in Cairo after defeating the Seventh Crusade, founding a regime that would defend the Islamic core for centuries.

name: Berke Khan Converts to Islam
desc: Berke Khan of the Golden Horde becomes the first major Mongol ruler to embrace Islam, setting the stage for confrontation with his cousin Hulagu.

name: Hulagu's Mission to Conquer the Islamic Heartland
desc: The Great Khan Mongke dispatches his brother Hulagu westward with orders to subdue the Ismaili Assassins and the Abbasid caliphate.

name: Vizier Ibn al-Alkami Disbands Baghdad's Army
desc: Caliph al-Mustasim's Shia vizier (later accused of Mongol collusion) advises drastic cuts to the Abbasid army on the eve of the Mongol assault.

name: Sack of Baghdad
desc: Hulagu Khan's army breaches Baghdad's walls and unleashes a forty-day sack. Estimates of the dead range from 200,000 to 800,000.

name: Destruction of the House of Wisdom
desc: Books from the Bayt al-Hikma are reportedly thrown into the Tigris until the river ran black with ink, in one of the largest single losses of knowledge in human history.

name: End of the Abbasid Caliphate
desc: Caliph al-Mustasim is executed by being rolled in a carpet and trampled by horses, ending five centuries of Abbasid rule and leaving the Sunni world without a symbolic head.

name: Battle of Ain Jalut
desc: The Mamluks under Sultan Qutuz and Baybars defeat a Mongol detachment in Galilee, the first major battlefield defeat of the Mongols and a turning point of world history.

name: Al-Azhar Becomes Premier Center of Islamic Learning
desc: Following Baghdad's destruction, the Mamluks revive Al-Azhar as a Sunni institution. It becomes the leading center of Islamic scholarship for centuries.

name: Fall of Acre
desc: The Mamluks capture Acre, the last major Crusader stronghold, ending nearly two centuries of Latin Christian polities in the Levant.

name: Ghazan Khan Converts to Islam
desc: The Ilkhan Ghazan converts to Islam and makes it the state religion of the Ilkhanate, a generation after his ancestors had destroyed Baghdad.

name: Osman I Founds the Ottoman Dynasty
desc: A small Turkic frontier principality (beylik) under Osman in northwest Anatolia begins absorbing Byzantine territory, planting the seed of the Ottoman Empire.

name: Ibn Taymiyya's Political Theology
desc: The Hanbali jurist responds to the trauma of Mongol invasions by developing a theology of resistance to nominally Muslim rulers who fail to apply Islamic law.

name: Mansa Musa's Pilgrimage to Mecca
desc: The Mali emperor's lavish hajj caravan distributes so much gold in Cairo that it depresses the Egyptian gold price for years, advertising West African wealth to the Old World.

name: Timbuktu and Sankore
desc: Mansa Musa develops Timbuktu into a major center of Islamic learning, with the Sankore mosque-university attracting scholars from across the Muslim world.

name: Ibn Battuta's Travels
desc: The Moroccan jurist sets out for hajj and ultimately spends nearly thirty years traveling across the Islamic world from West Africa to China.

name: Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddimah
desc: Ibn Khaldun composes the Muqaddimah, founding the disciplines of historiography, sociology, and economics on the concept of asabiyyah (group cohesion).

name: Battle of Kosovo
desc: Ottoman forces under Murad I defeat a Serbian-led coalition in Kosovo Polje, securing Ottoman dominance in the Balkans despite the death of the sultan.

name: Timur's Invasions
desc: Timur (Tamerlane) launches a series of devastating campaigns from his capital at Samarkand, sacking Delhi in 1398 and crushing the Ottomans at Ankara in 1402.

name: Conquest of Constantinople
desc: Sultan Mehmed II captures Constantinople with massive cannons, ending the Byzantine Empire and converting Hagia Sophia into a mosque. The event reverberates across Eurasia.

name: Songhai Empire Rises
desc: Sunni Ali consolidates the Songhai Empire on the upper Niger; under Askia Muhammad it becomes a model Islamic state with a famous hajj of its own.

name: Fall of Granada
desc: Ferdinand and Isabella accept the surrender of Granada from Muhammad XII (Boabdil), ending nearly 800 years of Muslim political presence in Iberia.

name: Expulsion of Muslims and Jews from Iberia
desc: The Alhambra Decree expels Sephardic Jews from Castile and Aragon; Muslims face forced conversion or expulsion in the following decades.

name: Ottomans Welcome Sephardic Jews
desc: Sultan Bayezid II dispatches the Ottoman navy to evacuate expelled Sephardic Jews, settling them in Istanbul, Salonika, Cairo, and other cities.

name: Columbus's Voyage Using Islamic Science
desc: Columbus sails with Arabic-derived navigational tools, star tables, and mathematical methods that descended from Andalusian and Abbasid scholarship.

name: Shah Ismail Founds the Safavid Empire
desc: The young Shah Ismail proclaims Twelver Shia Islam the official faith of his new Persian empire, fundamentally reshaping the religious map of the Middle East.

name: Battle of Chaldiran
desc: Ottoman firearms and artillery decisively defeat the Safavid cavalry of Shah Ismail, halting Safavid westward expansion and confirming a permanent Sunni-Shia geopolitical divide.

name: Selim I Conquers Mamluk Egypt
desc: Sultan Selim I defeats the Mamluk Sultanate at Marj Dabiq and Ridaniya, bringing Egypt, Syria, and the Hijaz under Ottoman rule.

name: Ottoman Caliphate Established
desc: With the Hijaz under Ottoman protection and Mamluk legitimacy absorbed, the Ottoman sultans assume the title of caliph and the role of guardians of the holy cities.

name: Reign of Suleiman the Magnificent
desc: Under Suleiman the Ottoman Empire reaches its peak in territory, military power, legal codification (Kanun), and architectural achievement (Sinan).

name: Battle of Mohacs
desc: Suleiman annihilates the Hungarian army in two hours, killing King Louis II and opening Central Europe to Ottoman influence.

name: Mughal Empire Founded
desc: Babur, a descendant of both Timur and Genghis Khan, defeats the Delhi Sultanate at Panipat with field artillery, founding the Mughal Empire.

name: First Siege of Vienna
desc: Suleiman's forces besiege Vienna but withdraw at the onset of winter, marking the high-water mark of Ottoman expansion into Central Europe.

name: Ottoman Rebuilding of Jerusalem Walls
desc: Suleiman commissions the rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls (still standing today) and refurbishes the Dome of the Rock with the famous blue Iznik tile work.

name: Reign of Akbar the Great
desc: The Mughal emperor Akbar consolidates a multi-religious empire across most of the Indian subcontinent, abolishing the jizya and integrating Hindu nobles into the administration.
